---
title: "Native Jupyter Notebook Support Has Arrived in Positron"
description: |
  Positron now includes a native Jupyter Notebook Editor in alpha, built from the ground up for data science workflows. Here's what's in the alpha, what's new in the March release, and how to get started.
categories:
  - Jupyter
  - Notebooks
author:
  - Cindy Tong
date: "2026-03-11"
image: positron-notebook-editor.png
---

Positron now ships with a native [Jupyter Notebook Editor](/positron-notebook-editor.qmd), and we want you to try it.

This is an early alpha release. The core experience is working well, especially for Python, and R support is actively being refined. We're releasing now because we'd rather build this with the community than wait for perfection. Your feedback will shape where it goes next.

{{< video https://rstudio.wistia.com/medias/tuwfndloue >}}

## Why we built our own notebook editor

Up to this point, Positron used the same Code OSS notebook editor that powers VS Code. That editor is solid for general-purpose development, but it wasn't designed with data science workflows in mind. The tradeoffs show up in small ways that compound over time: limited context for AI assistance, no deep integration with your variables or data, and a user experience that treats `.ipynb` files as just another file type.

We wanted notebooks to feel like a first-class part of a data science IDE, so we built our own.

![Positron Notebook Editor Layout](positron-notebook-layout.png)

## What's included out of the box

The Positron Notebook Editor brings the core capabilities of Positron directly into your notebook workflow:

**Variables Pane**: Variables update in real time as you run cells. No need to print or inspect manually.

**Data Explorer**: When a cell returns a Pandas or Polars DataFrame, you get an inline data viewer. Open the full Data Explorer to sort, filter, and profile your data. Any filtering or cleaning you do can be converted into code, so your analysis stays reproducible without writing repetitive `df.head()` or `df.describe()` calls.

{{<video https://rstudio.wistia.com/medias/tuwfndloue>}}

**AI Assistant**: The Assistant has access to your notebook's full context, including cell states, execution history, and outputs like images and tables. It can suggest edits, reorder cells, and run code with your permission. You can inspect exactly what context it's using and follow along as it works.

{{<video https://rstudio.wistia.com/medias/lmn2gtwjff>}}

**Help Pane**: Python and R documentation is available inline, with hyperlinks, without switching to a browser.

{{<video https://rstudio.wistia.com/medias/9qa1qii1vw>}}

**Publisher**: Deploy your `.ipynb` notebooks directly to Connect or Connect Cloud, where you can manage access, schedule runs, and view telemetry.

{{<video https://rstudio.wistia.com/medias/mrkp6grwiy>}}

## What's new in the March release

The March 2026 release focuses on smoothing the edit and execution loop as you move from exploration to a shareable artifact.

The biggest addition is Data Explorer integrated directly into cell outputs. When you run a cell that returns a DataFrame, the data viewer appears inline. This removes a common pattern in exploratory notebooks where scratch cells pile up just to look at intermediate data. You can explore without polluting your notebook, and when you find something worth keeping, generate the code for it.

A typical workflow in the Positron Notebook Editor now looks like this:

1. Import your data using Pandas or Polars. Variables update in the pane as cells run.
2. Explore your DataFrame in the inline Data Explorer. Sort and filter without writing any code.
3. Use the Assistant to generate a visualization based on your filtered data.
4. When the analysis is ready to share, use an AI action to add markdown headers and notes.
5. Publish the notebook to Connect or Connect Cloud.

## What's coming next

The roadmap includes SQL support, improved version control, R improvements and more. You can view and vote on items in the [GitHub roadmap](https://github.com/posit-dev/positron/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20state%3Aopen%20label%3A%22area%3A%20notebooks-jupyter%22).

## Join our upcoming workshop

On **Wednesday, March 25 at 11am EDT**, we're running a free workshop that walks through moving a dataset from exploration to production using the Positron Notebook Editor. It's a good way to see the full workflow end to end.

[Sign up here](https://events.zoom.us/ev/Ajss5j9VeRMe0zw-AtFKf7AUAsthzYhaYjYPeEEu1uYAQe1K0ud1~Agb_hSt1UGxd9fUyIDC32e6jAdEtbl7G_AaLZUYhRvyZR5cGpY5Kp_A0-w)

## Get started with the alpha

1. [Download Positron](https://positron.posit.co/) and install a release from February 2026 or later.
2. Enable the alpha by setting `positron.notebook.enabled` to `true` in your settings.
3. Try the [tutorial repository](https://github.com/posit-dev/positron-demos-notebooks) for examples that use the new features.
4. Share feedback in [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/posit-dev/positron/discussions) or [book time to talk with us directly](https://scheduler.zoom.us/cindy-tong/improving-the-positron-notebook-experience).
